content:
# Acts Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 226-227 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_002_page_001.jpg, ACT_002_page_002.jpg

---

### The Day of Pentecost (vv. 1-13)

**[1]** When the day of Pentecost arrived, they were all together in one place. **[2]** Suddenly a sound came from heaven like a violent, rushing wind, and it filled the entire house where they were sitting. **[3]** Then they saw what looked like tongues of fire that separated and came to rest on each one of them. **[4]** They were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other languages as the Spirit gave them the ability.

**[5]** Now there were devout Jews staying in Jerusalem from every nation under heaven. **[6]** When this sound rang out, a crowd gathered, and they were bewildered — because each one heard them speaking in his own native language. **[7]** Utterly amazed, they asked, "Are not all these people who are speaking Galileans? **[8]** Then how is it that each of us hears them in the language we were born into? **[9]** Parthians, Medes, and Elamites; residents of Mesopotamia, Judea and Cappadocia, Pontus and Asia, **[10]** Phrygia and Pamphylia, Egypt and the parts of Libya near Cyrene; visitors from Rome — **[11]** both Jews and converts — Cretans and Arabs! We hear them declaring the mighty works of God in our own languages!"

**[12]** They were all astonished and perplexed, saying to one another, "What can this mean?" **[13]** But others sneered and said, "They have had too much sweet wine."

### Peter's Sermon (vv. 14-36)

**[14]** Then Peter stood up with the Eleven, raised his voice, and addressed the crowd: "Fellow Jews and all of you who are staying in Jerusalem — listen carefully and let me explain this to you. **[15]** These people are not drunk, as you suppose. It is only nine in the morning! **[16]** No — this is what the prophet Joel was talking about:

**[17]** *'In the last days, God says,*
*I will pour out my Spirit on all people.*
*Your sons and daughters will prophesy,*
*your young men will see visions,*
*your old men will dream dreams.*
**[18]** *Even on my servants, both men and women,*
*I will pour out my Spirit in those days,*
*and they will prophesy.*
**[19]** *I will show wonders in the heavens above*
*and signs on the earth below —*
*blood and fire and clouds of smoke.*
**[20]** *The sun will turn to darkness*
*and the moon to blood*
*before the coming of the great and glorious day of the Lord.*
**[21]** *And everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.'*

**[22]** "People of Israel, hear me out: Jesus of Nazareth was a man endorsed by God among you through miracles, wonders, and signs that God performed through him in your midst — as you yourselves know. **[23]** This man was handed over to you by God's deliberate plan and foreknowledge, and you, with the help of lawless men, nailed him to a cross and killed him. **[24]** But God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because death could not possibly keep its grip on him.

**[25]** David says about him:

*'I kept the Lord always before me.*
*Because he is at my right hand,*
*I will not be shaken.*
**[26]** *Therefore my heart is glad and my tongue rejoices;*
*my body too will live in hope,*
**[27]** *because you will not abandon my soul to the grave,*
*nor will you let your Holy One see decay.*
**[28]** *You have shown me the paths of life;*
*you will fill me with joy in your presence.'*

**[29]** "Brothers, I can tell you plainly that the patriarch David died and was buried, and his tomb is still here among us to this day. **[30]** But he was a prophet, and he knew that God had sworn an oath to him — that from his own descendants, God would seat one on his throne. **[31]** Seeing what was coming, David spoke about the resurrection of the Messiah: that he would not be abandoned to the grave, and his body would not see decay.

**[32]** "This Jesus — God has raised him to life, and every one of us is a witness to it. **[33]** Exalted to God's right hand, he has received from the Father the promised Holy Spirit, and he has poured out what you now see and hear. **[34]** David did not ascend into heaven himself, yet he says:

*'The Lord said to my Lord:*
*Sit at my right hand*
**[35]** *until I make your enemies*
*a footstool for your feet.'*

**[36]** "So let all Israel know this beyond any doubt: God has made this Jesus — the very one you crucified — both Lord and Messiah."

### The Crowd Responds (vv. 37-41)

**[37]** When the people heard this, it pierced them to the heart. They cried out to Peter and the other apostles, "Brothers, what should we do?"

**[38]** Peter answered them, "Turn your lives around, and be baptized — every one of you — in the name of Jesus the Messiah, so that your sins may be forgiven. And you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit. **[39]** This promise is for you, and for your children, and for everyone who is far away — for everyone the Lord our God calls to himself."

**[40]** With many other words he urged them and pleaded with them: "Save yourselves from this corrupt generation!" **[41]** Those who embraced his message were baptized, and about three thousand people were added to their number that day.

### The First Community of Believers (vv. 42-47)

**[42]** They devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching, to the fellowship, to the breaking of bread, and to prayer. **[43]** A deep sense of awe came over everyone, and the apostles performed many wonders and signs. **[44]** All the believers stayed together and shared everything they had. **[45]** They would sell their possessions and property and distribute the money to anyone who had need. **[46]** Every day they met together in the temple courts with united hearts. They broke bread in each other's homes and shared their meals with glad and sincere hearts, **[47]** praising God and enjoying the goodwill of all the people. And day by day the Lord kept adding to their number those who were being saved.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Translation Philosophy for Acts 2
Acts 2 is the turning point of the entire book — the moment when the promise of Acts 1:8 is fulfilled and the early church is born. The chapter moves through four distinct registers: dramatic narrative (Pentecost), rhetorical address (Peter's sermon), urgent dialogue (the crowd's response), and pastoral description (the first community). I have worked to let each section carry its own energy and rhythm.

### Pentecost Narrative (vv. 1-13)
The Sinaiticus text on PDF page 226 (columns 2-3) preserves this passage clearly. The Greek HXOC WCΠEP ΦEPOMENHC ΠNOHC BIAIAC ("a sound like a rushing violent breath/wind") is vivid sensory language. I rendered this with "violent, rushing wind" to preserve the force. The word ΠNOH (breath/wind) echoes ΠNEYMA (Spirit) — both share the root idea of breath. This wordplay, central to the chapter's meaning, is difficult to capture in English but underlies my choice of "PNEUMA" as a translation identity.

### The Joel Quotation (vv. 17-21)
The Sinaiticus reads EN TAIC ECXATAIC HMERAIC ("in the last days") where the Hebrew of Joel 3:1 (LXX 2:28) has META TAYTA ("after these things"). The Sinaiticus aligns with the Acts tradition of interpreting Joel eschatologically. I have rendered this as poetry to reflect its prophetic register. The phrase ENΩΠION KYPIOY ("glorious" — literally "before the Lord") is rendered as "glorious" following the LXX tradition preserved in the manuscript.

### Peter's Rhetoric (vv. 22-36)
Peter's sermon is masterful ancient rhetoric — moving from shared knowledge ("as you yourselves know," v. 22) through scriptural argument to an explosive conclusion (v. 36). The Sinaiticus reading of v. 23 preserves EKΔOTON ("handed over") with ΔIA XEIPOC ANOMΩN ("through the hands of lawless men") — Rome is unnamed but unmistakable. I kept "lawless men" rather than specifying Romans, following the manuscript's own rhetorical strategy.

The climax in v. 36 — ACΦAΛΩC OYN ΓINΩCKETΩ ΠAC OIKOC ICPAΗΛ — carries the force of a legal verdict. I rendered ACΦAΛΩC as "beyond any doubt" rather than the more wooden "assuredly" to give English readers the same sense of finality the Greek audience would have felt.

### "Cut to the Heart" (v. 37)
The Greek KATENYΓHCAN THN KAPΔIAN ("they were pierced in the heart") is visceral — the verb KATANYCCOMAI means to be stabbed or pierced. "It pierced them to the heart" preserves this physical metaphor better than the common "they were cut to the heart," which has become somewhat domesticated in English.

### "Turn Your Lives Around" (v. 38)
I rendered METANOHCATE as "Turn your lives around" rather than the traditional "Repent." The Greek METANOIA denotes a complete change of mind and direction — not merely feeling sorry. "Turn your lives around" captures the active, total reorientation that Peter demands. This is one of the most significant dynamic choices in the chapter.

### The First Community (vv. 42-47)
The Sinaiticus text on PDF page 227 (columns 3-4) preserves this beloved passage clearly. The phrase TH KΛACEI TOY APTOY ("the breaking of bread") I have left as "the breaking of bread" since this has become recognizable even in English — it denotes both shared meals and the early Eucharist. The final verse's TOYC CΩZOMENOYC ("those being saved") uses the present participle — salvation as an ongoing reality, not a completed event. I rendered this as "those who were being saved" to preserve this continuous aspect.

### Manuscript Notes
The Sinaiticus text of Acts 2 spans from the second column of PDF page 226 through the third column of PDF page 227. The text is well-preserved with clear letter forms throughout. The scribe uses standard Sinaiticus conventions: continuous uncial script (scriptio continua), nomina sacra abbreviations for sacred names (ΘC for ΘEOC, KC for KYPIOC, ΠNA for ΠNEYMA, IC for IHCOYC, XPC for XPICTOC), and occasional corrections visible in the margins. No significant textual variants from the mainstream tradition were noted in this passage.

— PNEUMA
